title: Tagliatelle ai Funghi
subtitle: Fresh pasta with chestnut mushrooms, dried porcini, and cream
description: The combination of fresh chestnut mushrooms and rehydrated dried porcini gives this sauce two dimensions of mushroom flavour: the fresh chestnut mushrooms provide bulk and texture, while the soaking liquor from the dried porcini acts as a concentrated stock that carries through the whole dish. The result is earthy and deeply savoury. A splash of cream at the end brings everything together without overwhelming the mushrooms.
sourceNotes: A standard of Northern Italian pasta cookery, particularly in Piedmont and Emilia-Romagna where porcini mushrooms are abundant in autumn. No single canonical source; the combination of fresh and dried mushrooms appears consistently across Italian regional cookery.

recipeTools.notes: Wide enough to sauté the mushrooms without crowding.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wild cep mushrooms (Boletus edulis), dried and sold in small packets. The soaking water is as valuable as the mushrooms themselves — strain and use it as a stock.
The browned, sticky residue left in the pan after cooking. Deglazing with wine or stock lifts the fond and carries all its flavour into the sauce.

ingredients.notes: Soaked in 150 ml of boiling water for 15 minutes

steps: Place the dried porcini in a small bowl and cover with 150 ml of boiling water. Leave for 15 minutes. Lift out the porcini with a spoon, squeeze gently, and chop roughly. Pour the soaking liquor through a fine sieve or kitchen paper to remove any grit. Reserve both.
Bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil.

Set a large frying pan over a high heat with the olive oil and butter. Once the butter foams, add the chestnut mushrooms in a single layer. Cook without stirring for 3–4 minutes until browned on one side, then toss and cook for a further 2 minutes. Remove to a plate.
Reduce the heat to medium. In the same pan, cook the shallots for 3 minutes until soft, then add the garlic and thyme for 1 minute.

Add the rehydrated porcini and cook for 1 minute, then pour in the white wine and let it bubble to lift the fond from the base. Add the strained porcini soaking liquor and simmer for 3 minutes until reduced by half.
Return the chestnut mushrooms to the pan. Add the cream and cook for 2 minutes until the sauce just thickens. Season.

Cook the tagliatelle in the boiling water according to the packet. Reserve a ladle of pasta water, then drain. Add the tagliatelle to the sauce with tongs, tossing with a splash of pasta water if needed.
Remove from the heat. Add the parsley, toss, and serve with grated Parmesan.

content.text: — they need 15 minutes in boiling water. The soaking liquid, once strained of grit, is used as the sauce's stock base. Do not discard it.

Set a large frying pan over a high heat with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and {{butter-unsalted}} of butter. Once the butter foams, add {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of chestnut mushrooms in a single layer. Cook without stirring for 3–4 minutes until well browned, then toss and cook for 2 minutes more. Remove to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Cook the shallots in the same pan for 3 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and thyme leaves for 1 minute, then add the chopped rehydrated porcini. Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and stir to lift the

from the base. Add the strained porcini soaking liquor and simmer for 3 minutes until reduced by half. Return the chestnut mushrooms to the pan, add {{double-cream}} of cream, and cook for 2 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly. Season with salt and black pepper.

Cook {{tagliatelle-dried}} of tagliatelle in well-salted boiling water according to the packet. Reserve a ladle of pasta water, then drain. Add the tagliatelle to the sauce, tossing with tongs and adding a splash of pasta water if the sauce is thick. Remove from the heat. Scatter over the parsley and serve with grated Parmesan.
